title: Indirect taxes & subsidies
summary:
## The big idea When a market produces 'too much' of something (cigarettes, petrol) or 'too little' (vaccines, public transport), the government's favourite market-based fix is to change the price signal itself: **tax** what it wants less of, **subsidise** what it wants more of. This chapter is where demand, supply, elasticity and surplus all come together — and it is one of the most heavily examined topics at AS. ## Indirect taxes An **indirect tax** is a tax on **spending**, collected from sellers and (partly) passed on to buyers in higher prices — unlike a *direct* tax on income. Governments levy them to raise revenue and to discourage demerit goods and negative externalities. Two types, distinguished by the *shape* of the supply shift: - **Specific (unit) tax** — a fixed amount per unit, like \$2 per litre of fuel. Supply shifts up by the same vertical distance at every quantity: a **parallel shift**. - **Ad valorem tax** — a percentage of price, like VAT at 15%. The tax is bigger on dearer units, so the new supply curve **pivots away** from the old one (the gap widens). Either way the story is the same: supply shifts **up/left**, the equilibrium **price rises** and **quantity falls**. ## Who really pays? The incidence of a tax Here is the exam's favourite twist: the shop hands the tax to the government, but that doesn't mean the shop *bears* it. The **incidence (burden)** of the tax is how it is split between consumers (through a higher price paid) and producers (through a lower price received) — and it is decided entirely by **relative elasticities**: - **Inelastic demand** (or elastic supply) → **consumers** bear most: price rises nearly the full tax and quantity barely falls. - **Elastic demand** (or inelastic supply) → **producers** bear most: they cannot pass the tax on without losing their customers.
**What each part means:** the tax shifts supply from the solid **S** to the dashed **S+tax** — the vertical gap between them is the tax per unit. Output falls from **Q\*** to **Q1**. Consumers now pay **Pc** (higher than the old price **P\***) — the stretch from P\* up to Pc is the **consumer's share** of the tax. Producers keep only **Pp** after handing over the tax — the stretch from P\* down to Pp is the **producer's share**. The **shaded orange rectangle** (tax per unit × Q1) is **government revenue**; the little triangle between the curves from Q1 to Q\* is the **deadweight loss**.
This is why governments tax tobacco and fuel so heavily: demand is **inelastic**, so quantity falls little, revenue is large and reliable — and most of the burden lands on consumers. ## Subsidies A **subsidy** is the mirror image: a payment from the government to producers (or consumers) that **lowers price and raises output**. Used to encourage merit goods and positive externalities (public transport, renewable energy, education) or to support farm incomes. - Supply shifts **down/right**: equilibrium **price falls**, **quantity rises**. - The **benefit is shared** — consumers gain from the lower price, producers from a higher price received — again split by **relative elasticities**. - The **cost to government** = subsidy per unit × new quantity, a rectangle on the diagram — and an **opportunity cost**: that money can't build hospitals. ## Evaluation — the standard lines - **Information problem:** to fully correct an externality the tax/subsidy must equal its value — which nobody can measure precisely. Set it wrong and you swap one distortion for another. - **Inelastic demand blunts taxes:** a sugar tax raises money but may barely cut sugar consumption in the short run. - **Indirect taxes are regressive** — the poor spend a larger share of income on them — and high taxes can breed **black markets** (smuggled cigarettes). - **Subsidies are expensive**, can be captured by producers instead of reaching consumers, and risk propping up inefficient firms (dependency). ## Real-world application VAT/GST is the workhorse revenue tax in over 170 countries. Egypt, India and Indonesia have all wrestled with cutting costly **fuel subsidies** that mostly benefited better-off drivers. The UK's sugary drinks levy (2018) pushed manufacturers to reformulate recipes — a tax working through *supply* behaviour, not just demand.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Indirect tax** = tax on spending. **Specific** = fixed per unit → parallel shift; **ad valorem** = % of price → diverging pivot. - Tax → S shifts up → **P↑, Q↓**. Subsidy → S shifts down → **P↓, Q↑**. - **Incidence by elasticity:** inelastic demand → consumers bear most; elastic demand → producers bear most. - **Government revenue** = tax × new Q (rectangle); **deadweight loss** = triangle between old and new Q. - **Subsidy cost** = subsidy × new Q — always mention the opportunity cost. - Evaluate: information problem, regressive burden, black markets, producer capture, dependency.

keyTerms.definition: A tax on spending, collected by firms and passed on to consumers in higher prices.
A fixed tax per unit, causing a parallel upward shift of supply.
A percentage tax on price, causing supply to pivot up by a growing amount.
The division of the tax burden between consumers and producers, set by relative elasticities.
A government payment to lower price and raise output of a good.
The welfare loss from output moving away from the efficient equilibrium after a tax.

diagrams: **Specific tax:** supply shifts up by a constant vertical distance (S → S+tax); mark new higher price, lower quantity, and split the tax gap into consumer and producer incidence.
**Ad valorem tax:** supply pivots upward (widening gap); otherwise labelled like the specific-tax diagram.
**Tax revenue & deadweight loss:** shade the rectangle (tax × Q) as revenue and the triangle as deadweight loss.
**Subsidy:** supply shifts down/right; mark lower price, higher quantity, and the government cost rectangle.
tips: Distinguish **specific vs ad valorem** by the *shape* of the supply shift (parallel vs diverging).
Link **incidence to elasticity**: inelastic demand → consumer bears more. This is a very common exam point.
Explain why demerit goods are taxed (inelastic demand → high revenue, small quantity fall) — a ready evaluation line.
For subsidies, always state the **cost to government / opportunity cost** and who benefits most (by elasticity).
Evaluate the **information problem** — setting the ‘right’ tax/subsidy to fully correct an externality is difficult.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear tax/subsidy diagrams with incidence analysis.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Excellent on tax incidence and elasticity links.

revisionNotes:
# Indirect taxes & subsidies — quick revision

**Indirect tax basics**
- Tax on **spending**, collected via sellers. Supply shifts **up/left** → P↑, Q↓.
- **Specific (unit)** tax → parallel shift. **Ad valorem** (%) tax → diverging pivot.

**Incidence (who bears it)**
- Split between consumers (P\* → Pc) and producers (P\* → Pp), decided by **relative elasticities**.
- **Inelastic demand / elastic supply → consumers bear most** (tobacco, fuel).
- Elastic demand / inelastic supply → producers bear most.
- Revenue = tax × new Q (rectangle). Deadweight loss = triangle between Q1 and Q\*.

**Subsidies**
- Payment to producers/consumers → supply shifts **down/right** → P↓, Q↑.
- Benefit shared by elasticity; **cost = subsidy × new Q** → opportunity cost for the budget.

**Evaluation checklist**
- Information problem — right size of tax/subsidy unknowable.
- Inelastic demand → tax raises revenue but barely cuts quantity.
- Regressive effect on the poor; black-market risk.
- Subsidies: expensive, may breed inefficiency/dependency, captured by producers.

**Exam habit**
- Draw the diagram, label Pc / P\* / Pp / Q1 / Q\*, shade revenue, and *always* link incidence to elasticity.
questions:
# Practice questions — Indirect taxes & subsidies **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A specific (unit) tax differs from an ad valorem tax because a specific tax: - A — shifts the supply curve up by the same vertical distance at every output - B — shifts the supply curve up by a larger amount at higher prices - C — is paid by producers while ad valorem taxes are paid by consumers - D — shifts the demand curve rather than the supply curve
▸ Show answer

A. A fixed amount per unit gives a parallel shift; a percentage tax gives a widening gap (pivot). Who pays depends on elasticities, not the tax type (C is wrong).

**2.** Demand for a good is highly price inelastic and supply is elastic. An indirect tax on the good will be borne: - A — mostly by producers - B — mostly by consumers - C — equally by both - D — entirely by the government
▸ Show answer

B. With inelastic demand, sellers can pass the tax on: price rises by most of the tax and quantity barely falls, so consumers carry most of the burden.

**3.** A subsidy is granted to producers of solar panels. The most likely result is: - A — price rises and quantity falls - B — price falls and quantity rises - C — price and quantity both fall - D — supply shifts left
▸ Show answer

B. A subsidy cuts production costs, shifting supply right/down: equilibrium price falls and quantity rises.

**4.** After a \$3 per unit tax, equilibrium quantity is 400 units. Government revenue from the tax is: - A — \$1,200 - B — \$400 - C — \$3 - D — impossible to calculate without the price
▸ Show answer

A. Revenue = tax per unit × quantity actually traded = \$3 × 400 = \$1,200 — the rectangle on the diagram. The price level isn't needed.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A government imposes a specific tax of \$0.50 per litre on sugary drinks. Over the next year the retail price rises from \$2.00 to \$2.40 per litre, and sales fall from 10 million to 9 million litres. A health minister calls it 'a tax on obesity'; manufacturers say it 'unfairly punishes the poorest families'. **(a)** Define the 'incidence' of a tax. **[2]** **(b)** Using the data, calculate how the tax is shared between consumers and producers. **[4]** **(c)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, the effect of the tax on consumer surplus and producer surplus. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the tax is likely to achieve its aim of reducing sugar consumption. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) How the burden of a tax is divided between consumers and producers. [1+1]

(b) Price paid by consumers rose \$0.40 of the \$0.50 tax → consumers bear \$0.40 (80%); producers receive \$2.40 − \$0.50 = \$1.90, down \$0.10 from \$2.00 → producers bear \$0.10 (20%). Implies demand is relatively inelastic. [working 2, split 1, comment 1]

(c) S shifts up by \$0.50; Q falls 10m → 9m; CS shrinks (higher price, fewer litres), PS shrinks (lower net price); government revenue \$0.50 × 9m = \$4.5m; deadweight-loss triangle. Diagram with Pc/P*/Pp labelled. [6]

(d) For: quantity did fall 10%; long-run elasticity rises (habits change, reformulation as in the UK levy). Against: demand inelastic short run (PED ≈ −0.5 here: −10% ÷ +20%); substitution to other sugary snacks; regressive burden. Judgement: modest effect alone; stronger combined with information campaigns. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain, with the aid of diagrams, the difference between a specific tax and an ad valorem tax. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether indirect taxes are an effective way of discouraging the consumption of demerit goods. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Definitions; parallel shift vs diverging pivot drawn and explained; example of each (fuel duty; VAT).

(b) For: raises price → law of demand; revenue can fund health care/education campaigns. Against: inelastic demand → small quantity fall; regressive; black markets; information problem on the 'right' rate. Judgement weighing short vs long run and the good in question.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, how a subsidy to producers affects the market for a good. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether subsidising agriculture is a good use of a government's limited budget. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) S shifts down/right by the subsidy; P falls, Q rises; benefit split by elasticity; cost rectangle = subsidy × new Q.

(b) For: food security, stable farm incomes, positive externalities of rural employment. Against: opportunity cost (health, education), overproduction and waste, benefits captured by large landowners, trade distortion (link to A2 protectionism). Judgement: depends on design and targeting — e.g. Egypt's and India's food/fuel subsidy reforms.
